item.title: Re: SCP-4784: Nature Abhors a Vacuum Tube
item.content:encoded: Update! It's "The Not-Too-Distant Future", and our mismatched heroes are waking up to an unexpected mess. Unwinding may be contained, but the mystery is far from over.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ANOTHER steakshift piece. they just keep coming i love em

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh hey! It's cool to see this is up!

I'm super hyped to see the tales that add to this (which should really clear up some of my concerns here that I have already probably annoyed Tsercele enough with ;p); cos GAW and anart are some of my favourite things here :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I’m not currently reading those tales though, and I don’t see a strong indication of that incorrect analysis in the writing itself (though I may have glossed over something). The note at the end being wrong and forcing the reader to re-evaluate what they’ve already been told through a flawed lens is a more interesting idea, but you need to convey the need for that. Otherwise the note will almost always be taken at face value.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fair enough! Balance is difficult, and I wanted this to function as a standalone article, so it may be wrapped up a bit too neat… the Tales branching out from it will be very messy indeed. For what it's worth, Dr. Wilkes is not entirely correct about when and where the shotgun was fired, or at whom.

⬇️ Extremely good point! I buried the lingering questions around this anomaly a little too deeply. Fortunately, Penelope Gore is on the case… and she's still very confused. (Added a "deleted file" addendum!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This mirrors my feelings. The note at the end deflates anything that's built up throughout the narrative, and it isn't itself interesting enough to make up for that deflation. I don't feel particularly respected as a reader (not that I think you are personally being disrespectful) when things are just laid out neatly at the end, even if the neat note goes to lengths to describe it as something illogical and exotic. It's harder to lead a reader to the same conclusion, but I think that's the right way to go with this sort of thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed most of this, but was unimpressed by the infamous trope of explanatory note-at-the-end enough to flip to downvoting. This was otherwise well-crafted enough to deserve a more elegant storytelling device.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gosh, high praise! Thank you kindly. I'm glad you enjoyed the piece, and I'm really looking forward to exploring the next twists and turns.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you so much! It took a while to hit the proper balance of mystery vs. clarity, particularly with the pataphysics memo; this one is simple to contain, tough to explain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: From what I've seen, this is only your second SCP, and you've already made a huge impression on me as an author; I really think you have a lot of talent when it comes to writing, and this really shows. A well deserved +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a wonderfully compelling piece. There's enough new hooks to keep me engaged the whole way through, and all the twists and turns kept me guessing where it was headed. Especially enjoyed the Tilford addendum, along with the explanation — killer prose that did well in describing the anomaly behavior.

Easy +1.

item.title: SCP-4784: Nature Abhors a Vacuum Tube
item.content:encoded:
At its heart, this SCP is a juxtaposition of Canadiana: obscure historical texture from Ontario, fudged so far as to be unrecognizable, and David Cronenberg's "Videodrome" (1983). When I first watched that movie, I was struck by the absence of a clear boundary between "real" and "unreal". By the time Max Renn realizes what's going on, it's already too late; the signal is in his head, warping everything he perceives, and since the viewer can't be sure where the hallucinations begin and end, the center of the story is everywhere. It's also interwoven with reality: not only was "CIVIC-TV" was inspired by a real Toronto station, but Cronenberg was specifically anxious about tuning in late at night to something that was "definitely not for public consumption".

  • Thanks go out to kinchtheknifeblade for taking an early look, to cybersqyd for thorough draft critique, and to NatVoltaic for checking the final draft! Y'all are the best!
  • (If I've sourced it correctly,) "SCRAMBLE gear" is descended from Dr Dan's Incident 096-1-A.
  • Dr. Michelle Wilkes is a character featured in SCP-4869, by ManyMeats. I stumbled across this article while looking up pataphysics terminology, and Dr. Wilkes' expert opinion seemed like a natural fit!
  • The blooming rose in Unwinding's end card is visually modified from a photograph by Gayatri Priyadarshini, made available on Wikimedia Commons under CC Attribution-Share Alike 4.0.
  • I ran both title cards through PHOTOMOSH.
Spoilers and Easter Eggs
– hide block

(The Artist Formerly Known As) Anna Bojarski made something that was "definitely not for public consumption", and it endured after she (mostly) died. Ever since, Wren Masterson has been living with Anna's mistake… because it's also theirs. They've had some assistance for the past eleven years (Felix Kipp was a sly old man), but they couldn't figure out a solution… until someone else had an idea.

The Foundation doesn't know everything about this anomaly, but hey, neither does steakshift! Some of the inciting events haven't happened yet! Except they have, because it's not a time machine, it's a story, and from at least one perspective… the ending/beginning is already written.

  • "Luther Stark" is named after a henchman from the 1981 TV movie "Riding With Death", which was featured in Season 8 of Mystery Science Theater 3000. (Anna Bojarski is almost as elusive as Robert Denby!)
  • "It's a conundrum" is an allusion to The Ring, which helped Wren discover their powers in the first place. Like "public consumption", it's also a tell/slip: if they truly found the album at random, how would they know it depicts a killer TV?
  • There is an excessively subtle music reference embedded into Unwinding's aesthetic. Can you spot it?

Edit Jan. 9, 2020 — Re-edited Wilkes' memo and rewrote the finale!
